message:
fix(time_abi): per-arch vDSO symbol version + positive-rc fallback

The vDSO fast-path for clock_gettime/gettimeofday had two latent
portability bugs that surfaced when running on non-x86_64 targets:

1. The dlvsym() lookup hardcoded the symbol version "LINUX_2.6",
   which only exists on x86_64. On aarch64 the kernel tags the
   time vDSO symbols as "LINUX_2.6.39", and on riscv64 as
   "LINUX_4.15". The lookup silently returned NULL on those
   architectures, dropping every caller onto the slower direct
   syscall path with no diagnostic.
2. classify_vdso_return() treated any non-zero rc the same way —
   either negated it and wrote it to errno, or (for rc == -ENOSYS)
   fell back to syscall. But the Linux vDSO contract is that a
   *positive* return value also means "not implemented, fall back",
   not "errno value". The old logic would call set_abi_errno with
   a wildly negative number derived from that positive return,
   corrupting the caller's errno.

Refactor into a VdsoCallOutcome { Success, FallbackToSyscall,
Fail(c_int) } enum so the three cases are explicit and
exhaustively matched at every call site. Add a const
vdso_symbol_version_bytes() helper that picks the right version
string per target_arch, with a matching CStr wrapper used by both
dlvsym call sites.

Expose __frankenlibc_vdso_symbol_version_name() and
__frankenlibc_classify_vdso_return() as #[doc(hidden)] test hooks
so the companion test file can lock in the per-architecture
version string (x86_64=LINUX_2.6, aarch64=LINUX_2.6.39,
riscv64=LINUX_4.15) and verify the positive-rc / -ENOSYS /
negative-errno classification trio.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
